content.text: An abstract class is a class that cannot be instantiated directly. It defines a contract — the abstract methods that every concrete subclass must implement — while also providing shared, concrete implementations that subclasses inherit for free. This is the Template Method pattern: the abstract class defines the algorithm's skeleton; the subclasses fill in the steps.

attrs.alt: Abstract class Logger with abstract write() method and concrete info()/error() methods; ConsoleLogger and FileLogger implement write()

content.text: Abstract members are declared with the abstract keyword and have no body. Concrete subclasses must provide an implementation. TypeScript reports an error if the subclass does not implement all abstract members.

Both abstract classes and interfaces define contracts. The key differences are: abstract classes can have constructor logic, field initialisers, concrete method bodies, and access modifiers. Interfaces are purely structural and cannot hold implementation.
Abstract Base Service with Shared Logic
Abstract Class as a Token in DI
An abstract class — because it has a runtime representation — can be used as an Angular injection token. This lets you inject an interface-like contract without needing a separate InjectionToken.

Abstract classes are the idiomatic way to implement the Template Method pattern in TypeScript. The abstract class defines the algorithm skeleton in a concrete method; the abstract methods are the steps that vary between subclasses. This keeps the algorithm stable while allowing extension points.
Abstract classes can take type parameters. This pattern pairs naturally with Angular repositories or caches where the read/write strategy varies but the caching or pagination logic is shared.
Calling Abstract Methods from the Constructor
Never call an abstract method inside the abstract class constructor. At the point the base constructor runs, the subclass fields have not been initialised yet, causing subtle bugs.
If there is only ever one concrete subclass, an abstract class adds ceremony without benefit. Prefer a plain class until a second implementation appears — YAGNI applies to abstract hierarchies too.
To unit-test the shared logic in an abstract class, create a minimal concrete test double inside the test file rather than mocking the abstract class directly.
Testing Angular Services that Extend an Abstract Base
cannot be instantiated directly — they serve as base types that define a contract through abstract members and share concrete logic.
have no body and must be overridden by every concrete subclass. TypeScript reports an error if an implementation is missing.
over interface when you need shared field initialisers, concrete methods, a constructor, or private/protected state.
when you only need a type contract and classes from unrelated hierarchies need to satisfy it.
— the abstract class serves as both the contract and the injection key, making providers easy to swap.
— abstract class defines the algorithm, subclasses fill in the steps — is the canonical use case for abstract classes.
